description: Standard: profile of a forensic accountant

input.subject: James Okoro, 48, forensic accountant who has testified as an expert witness in three of the largest corporate fraud cases in the past decade
input.editorial_angle: Okoro works in the most abstract corner of white-collar crime — following money through shell companies and offshore structures. The profile explores what it means to spend your career reconstructing other people's lies, and what that does to your relationship with trust.
quotes: 'Numbers don't lie. But the people who arrange them absolutely do.'
'I have never testified in a case where I wasn't surprised by something. The money always goes somewhere you don't expect.'
'My wife says I audit the grocery receipt. She's not wrong.'
'The hardest part is not finding the fraud. The hardest part is explaining it to a jury that doesn't want to believe a person in a nice suit could steal $200 million.'
input.bio: Former Big Four auditor, left to start his own forensic accounting firm in 2016. PhD in accounting from a major university. Has been retained by the DOJ, SEC, and private litigants.
input.observational_details: Interview at his office. Sparse, orderly desk. Three monitors showing spreadsheets. A single framed photo of his two children. He speaks in complete, precise sentences. Rarely uses filler words.

input.subject: Rosa Delgado, community organizer
input.editorial_angle: Brief sidebar profile for a feature on neighborhood advocacy
quotes: 'Nobody listens until you show up at the meeting.'
'I've been doing this for 20 years and the problems are the same — it's the faces at the council table that change.'
input.bio: 20 years as a community organizer in the east side. No formal education in policy. Started after her apartment building was condemned.

description: European: profile of a Frontex whistleblower

input.subject: Nadia Kovacs (pseudonym), 36, former operations analyst at Frontex, the EU border agency
input.editorial_angle: Kovacs leaked internal documents showing that Frontex leadership was aware of illegal pushbacks of migrants at the Greek-Turkish border and chose not to intervene. She is now living under a new identity after losing her job and facing legal threats. The profile examines what it costs to do the right thing inside a European institution.
quotes: 'I saw the reports. Everyone saw the reports. The question was never whether it was happening. The question was whether anyone would say so.'
'I am not brave. I just could not keep pretending.'
'Europe talks about values. I believed that. I was naive.'
'My former colleagues do not speak to me. I understand. They have mortgages.'
input.bio: Hungarian-born, studied international relations in Vienna, joined Frontex in 2019, left in 2024 after filing an internal complaint that was dismissed
input.observational_details: Interview conducted over encrypted video call. She appears calm but speaks quickly when discussing the documents. Her apartment is visible behind her — sparse, rented, impersonal. She has moved three times since leaving Frontex.
